title: Romanesque Revival architecture

Unlike the historic Romanesque style, Romanesque Revival buildings tended to feature more simplified arches and windows than their historic counterparts.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Romanian Revival architecture](/wiki/Romanian_Revival_architecture "Romanian Revival architecture").

Style of building in 19th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/SmithsonianCastel_07120014.jpg/250px-SmithsonianCastel_07120014.jpg)](/wiki/File:SmithsonianCastel_07120014.jpg)

The [Smithsonian Institution Building](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution_Building "Smithsonian Institution Building"), an early example of American Romanesque Revival designed by [James Renwick Jr.](/wiki/James_Renwick_Jr. "James Renwick Jr.") in 1855

**Romanesque Revival** (or **Neo-Romanesque**) is a [style](/wiki/Architectural_style "Architectural style") of building employed beginning in the mid-19th century[[1]](#cite_note-1) inspired by the 11th- and 12th-century [Romanesque architecture](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"). Unlike the historic Romanesque style, Romanesque Revival buildings tended to feature more simplified arches and windows than their historic counterparts.

An early variety of Romanesque Revival style known as [Rundbogenstil](/wiki/Rundbogenstil "Rundbogenstil") ("Round-arched style") was popular in German lands and in the [German diaspora](/wiki/German_diaspora "German diaspora") beginning in the 1830s.[[2]](#cite_note-2) By far the most prominent and influential American [architect](/wiki/Architect "Architect") working in a free "Romanesque" manner was [Henry Hobson Richardson](/wiki/Henry_Hobson_Richardson "Henry Hobson Richardson"). In the United States, the style derived from examples set by him are termed [Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque"), of which not all are Romanesque Revival.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Romanesque Revival is also sometimes referred to as the "[Norman style](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture")" or "[Lombard style](/wiki/Lombard_Romanesque "Lombard Romanesque")", particularly in works published during the 19th century after variations of historic Romanesque that were developed by the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") in [England](/wiki/England "England") and by the Italians in [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), respectively. Like its influencing Romanesque style, the Romanesque Revival style was widely used for churches, and occasionally for synagogues such as the [New Synagogue of Strasbourg](/wiki/Synagogue_du_Quai_Kl%C3%A9ber "Synagogue du Quai Kléber") built in 1898, and the [Congregation Emanu-El of New York](/wiki/Congregation_Emanu-El_of_New_York "Congregation Emanu-El of New York") built in 1929.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The style was quite popular for university campuses in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, especially in the United States and Canada; well-known examples can be found at the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California"), [Tulane University](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University"), [University of Denver](/wiki/University_of_Denver "University of Denver"), [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), and [Wayne State University](/wiki/Wayne_State_University "Wayne State University").

## The Romanesque Revival or Norman Revival in Great Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanesque_Revival_architecture&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The Romanesque Revival or Norman Revival in Great Britain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Culzean_Castle_2013-09-03_17-15-37.jpg/250px-Culzean_Castle_2013-09-03_17-15-37.jpg)](/wiki/File:Culzean_Castle_2013-09-03_17-15-37.jpg)

[Culzean Castle](/wiki/Culzean_Castle "Culzean Castle") by Robert Adam, 1771

See also: [Romanesque Revival architecture in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture_in_the_United_Kingdom "Romanesque Revival architecture in the United Kingdom")

The development of the Norman revival style in the British Isles took place over a near two century period, starting with [Inigo Jones](/wiki/Inigo_Jones "Inigo Jones")'s refenestration of the White Tower of the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") in 1637–38 and work at Windsor Castle by [Hugh May](/wiki/Hugh_May "Hugh May") for King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), but this was little more than restoration work. In the 18th century, the use of round arched windows was thought of as being [Saxon](/wiki/Saxon "Saxon") rather than Norman, and examples of buildings with round arched windows include [Shirburn Castle](/wiki/Shirburn_Castle "Shirburn Castle") in Oxfordshire, Wentworth in Yorkshire, and [Enmore Castle](/wiki/Enmore_Castle "Enmore Castle") in Somerset. In Scotland the style started to emerge with the Duke of Argyl's castle at [Inverary](/wiki/Inverary "Inverary"), started in 1744, and castles by [Robert Adam](/wiki/Robert_Adam "Robert Adam") at [Culzean](/wiki/Culzean "Culzean") (1771), Oxenfoord (1780–82), Dalquharran, (1782–85) and [Seton Palace](/wiki/Seton_Palace "Seton Palace"), 1792. In England [James Wyatt](/wiki/James_Wyatt "James Wyatt") used round arched windows at [Sandleford Priory](/wiki/Sandleford_Priory_(monastery) "Sandleford Priory (monastery)"), Berkshire, in 1780–89 and the Duke of Norfolk started to rebuild [Arundel Castle](/wiki/Arundel_Castle "Arundel Castle"), while [Eastnor Castle](/wiki/Eastnor_Castle "Eastnor Castle") in Herefordshire was built by [Robert Smirke](/wiki/Robert_Smirke_(architect) "Robert Smirke (architect)") between 1812 and 1820.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

At this point, the Norman Revival became a recognisable architectural style. In 1817, [Thomas Rickman](/wiki/Thomas_Rickman "Thomas Rickman") published his *An Attempt to Discriminate the Styles of English Architecture from the Conquest To the Reformation*. It was now realised that 'round-arch architecture' was largely Romanesque in the British Isles and came to be described as Norman rather than Saxon.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The start of an "archaeologically correct" Norman Revival can be recognised in the architecture of [Thomas Hopper](/wiki/Thomas_Hopper_(architect) "Thomas Hopper (architect)"). His first attempt at this style began at [Gosford Castle](/wiki/Gosford_Castle "Gosford Castle") in Armagh in Ireland in 1819, but far more successful was his [Penrhyn Castle](/wiki/Penrhyn_Castle "Penrhyn Castle") near Bangor in North Wales built for the Pennant family between 1820 and 1837. The style did not catch on for domestic buildings, though many country houses and mock castles were built in the Castle Gothic or Castellated style during the [Victorian period](/wiki/Victorian_period "Victorian period"), which was a mixed Gothic style.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

However, the Norman Revival did catch on for church architecture. [Thomas Penson](/wiki/Thomas_Penson "Thomas Penson"), a Welsh architect, would have been familiar with Hopper's work at Penrhyn, who developed Romanesque Revival church architecture. Penson was influenced by French and Belgian Romanesque Revival architecture, and particularly the earlier Romanesque phase of German [Brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic"). At St David's Newtown, 1843–47, and St Agatha's Llanymynech, 1845, he copied the tower of [St. Salvator's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Salvator%27s_Cathedral "St. Salvator's Cathedral"), Bruges. Other examples of Romanesque revival by Penson are [Christ Church, Welshpool](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Welshpool "Christ Church, Welshpool"), 1839–1844, and the porch to Langedwyn Church. He was an innovator in his use of [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") to produce decorative Romanesque mouldings, saving on the expense of stonework.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Penson's last church in the Romanesque Revival style was [Rhosllannerchrugog](/wiki/Rhosllannerchrugog "Rhosllannerchrugog"), Wrexham, 1852.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The Romanesque adopted by Penson contrasts with the Italianate Romanesque of other architects such as [Thomas Henry Wyatt](/wiki/Thomas_Henry_Wyatt "Thomas Henry Wyatt"), who designed [Saint Mary and Saint Nicholas Church](/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_and_St_Nicholas,_Wilton "Church of St Mary and St Nicholas, Wilton"), in this style at [Wilton](/wiki/Wilton,_Wiltshire#Parish_church "Wilton, Wiltshire"), which was built between 1841 and 1844 for the Dowager Countess of Pembroke and her son, Lord Herbert of Lea.[[10]](#cite_note-10) During the 19th century, the architecture selected for Anglican churches depended on the churchmanship of particular congregations. Whereas high churches and [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic"), which were influenced by the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"), were built in [Gothic Revival architecture](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture"), low churches and broad churches of the period were often built in the Romanesque Revival style. Some of the later examples of this Romanesque Revival architecture is seen in Non-conformist or [Dissenting](/wiki/Dissenter "Dissenter") churches and chapels. A good example of this is by the Lincoln architects [Drury and Mortimer](/wiki/Michael_Drury "Michael Drury"), who designed the Mint Lane Baptist Chapel in Lincoln in a debased Italianate Romanesque revival style in 1870.[[11]](#cite_note-11) This style of Church architecture in Britain disappeared after about 1870, but was succeeded by [Byzantine Revival architecture](/wiki/Byzantine_Revival_architecture "Byzantine Revival architecture") in the early 20th century.

A notable exception to this decline was [Our Lady of Grace Church, Charlton](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Grace_Church,_Charlton "Our Lady of Grace Church, Charlton") (1905–1906), built by French architect Eugène-Jacques Gervais for the exiled [Sisters of the Assumption](/wiki/Religious_of_the_Assumption "Religious of the Assumption"). The church represents a rare example of authentic Continental Neo-Romanesque tradition transplanted to Britain during the French religious exile of 1901–1914, when approximately 30,000 French religious were forced into exile by anti-clerical legislation. Built of stock brick with stone dressings, the church features a five-bay nave with barrel vault and Corinthian columns with [scagliola](/wiki/Scagliola "Scagliola") shafts, demonstrating how diaspora communities preserved their architectural heritage while adapting to British contexts.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanesque_Revival_architecture&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/AlbanyNYCityHall.jpg/250px-AlbanyNYCityHall.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlbanyNYCityHall.jpg)

[Albany City Hall](/wiki/Albany_City_Hall "Albany City Hall") in [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), designed by [H. H. Richardson](/wiki/Henry_Hobson_Richardson "Henry Hobson Richardson"), the father of "[Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque"), 1880

The Church of the Pilgrims—now the [Maronite Cathedral of Our Lady of Lebanon](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Lebanon_Maronite_Cathedral_(Brooklyn) "Our Lady of Lebanon Maronite Cathedral (Brooklyn)")—in [Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn"), designed by [Richard Upjohn](/wiki/Richard_Upjohn "Richard Upjohn") and built 1844–1846, is generally considered the first work of Romanesque Revival architecture in the United States.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It was soon followed by a more prominent design for the [Smithsonian Institution Building](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution_Building "Smithsonian Institution Building") in [Washington, DC](/wiki/Washington,_DC "Washington, DC"), designed by [James Renwick Jr.](/wiki/James_Renwick_Jr. "James Renwick Jr."), and built 1847–1851. Renwick allegedly submitted two proposals to the design competition, one Gothic and the other Romanesque in the style. The Smithsonian chose the latter, which was based on designs from German architecture books.[[16]](#cite_note-Poppeliers-16)

Several concurrent forces contributed to the popularizing of the Romanesque Revival in the United States. The first was an influx of German immigrants in the 1840s, who brought the style of the Rundbogenstil with them.[[16]](#cite_note-Poppeliers-16) Second, a series of works on the style was published concurrently with the earliest built examples. The first of these, *Hints on Public Architecture*, written by social reformer [Robert Dale Owen](/wiki/Robert_Dale_Owen "Robert Dale Owen") in 1847–1848, was prepared for the Building Committee of the Smithsonian Institution and prominently featured illustrations of Renwick's Smithsonian Institution Building. Owen argued that [Greek Revival architecture](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture")—then the prevailing style in the United States for everything from churches to banks to private residences—was unsuitable as a national American style. He maintained that the [Greek temples](/wiki/Greek_temple "Greek temple") upon which the style was based had neither the windows, chimneys, nor stairs required by modern buildings, and that the low-pitched temple roofs and tall [colonnades](/wiki/Colonnade "Colonnade") were ill-adapted to cold northern climates. To Owen, most Greek Revival buildings thus lacked architectural truth, because they attempted to hide 19th-century necessities behind classical temple facades.[[17]](#cite_note-Owen-17) In its place, he offered that the Romanesque style was ideal for a more flexible and economic American architecture.[[18]](#cite_note-Meeks-18)

Soon after, the [Congregational Church](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") published *A Book of Plans for Churches and Parsonages* in 1853, containing 18 designs by 10 architects, including Upjohn, Renwick, [Henry Austin](/wiki/Henry_Austin_(architect) "Henry Austin (architect)"), and [Gervase Wheeler](/wiki/Gervase_Wheeler "Gervase Wheeler"), most in the Romanesque Revival style. [Richard Salter Storrs](/wiki/Richard_Salter_Storrs "Richard Salter Storrs") and other clergy on the book's committee were members or frequent preachers of Upjohn's Church of the Pilgrims.[[19]](#cite_note-Steege-19)
St. Joseph Church in [Hammond, Indiana](/wiki/Hammond,_Indiana "Hammond, Indiana"), is Romanesque Revival.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The most celebrated "Romanesque Revival" architect of the late 19th century was [H. H. Richardson](/wiki/Henry_Hobson_Richardson "Henry Hobson Richardson"), whose mature style was so individual that it is known as "[Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque")". Among his most prominent buildings are [Trinity Church (Boston)](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(Boston) "Trinity Church (Boston)") and [Sever Hall](/wiki/Sever_Hall "Sever Hall") and [Austin Hall](/wiki/Austin_Hall_(Harvard_University) "Austin Hall (Harvard University)") at Harvard University.

His disciple, [R.H. Robertson](/wiki/R._H._Robertson "R. H. Robertson"), worked in a similar style, designing the [Pequot Library](/wiki/Pequot_Library "Pequot Library"), [Shelburne Farms](/wiki/Shelburne_Farms "Shelburne Farms"), the [New York Savings Bank](/wiki/New_York_Savings_Bank_Building "New York Savings Bank Building"), and [Jackie Kennedy](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis")'s childhood home [Hammersmith Farm](/wiki/Hammersmith_Farm "Hammersmith Farm").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The [Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception") in Washington, D.C., the largest Catholic church in North America[[22]](#cite_note-Shrine-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) and one of the largest churches in the world,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] was begun in 1920 by contractor John McShain and completed in 2017.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
